elements.sent-2411-001: We present a novel technique to significantly reduce the offline cost associated to non-intrusive nonlinear tensors identification in reduced order models (ROMs) of geometrically nonlinear, finite elements (FE)-discretized structural dynamics problems.
elements.sent-2411-002: The ROM is obtained by Galerkin-projection of the governing equations on a reduction basis (RB) of Vibration Modes (VMs) and Static Modal Derivatives (SMDs), resulting in reduced internal forces that are cubic polynomial in the reduced coordinates.
elements.sent-2411-003: The unknown coefficients of the nonlinear tensors associated with this polynomial representation are identified using a modified version of Enhanced Enforced Displacement (EED) method which leverages Energy Conserving Sampling and Weighting (ECSW) as hyperreduction technique for efficiency improvement.
elements.sent-2411-004: Specifically, ECSW is employed to accelerate the evaluations of the nonlinear reduced tangent stiffness matrix that are required within EED.
elements.sent-2411-005: Simulation-free training sets of forces for ECSW are obtained from displacements corresponding to quasi-random samples of a nonlinear second order static displacement manifold.
elements.sent-2411-006: The proposed approach is beneficial for the investigation of the dynamic response of structures subjected to acoustic loading, where multiple VMs must be added in the RB, resulting in expensive nonlinear tensor identification.
elements.sent-2411-007: Superiority of the novel method over standard EED is demonstrated on FE models of a shallow curved clamped panel and of a nine-bay aeronautical reinforced panel modelled, using the commercial finite element program Abaqus.
elements.sent-2411-008: The investigation of the response of aerospace panels subjected to acoustic loading has been of great interest for the aerospace community in the past decades.
elements.sent-2411-009: Experimental \cite{Hollkamp2018 and computational studies \cite{Gordon2011,Spottswood2008,Przekop2007,przekop2006nonlinear,mignolet2013review have been performed with the end goal of predicting fatigue life and guarantee in service structural integrity.}}
elements.sent-2411-010: With the advent of the Finite Element (FE) method and the steady increase in computational power of modern computers, numerical investigations have become increasingly appealing compared to traditional experiments, which typically require costly testing facilities and instrumentation.
elements.sent-2411-011: However, vibration assessment of real-life components using FEM is generally computationally demanding, if not intractable.
elements.sent-2411-012: This is due to the complex geometries that require large meshes, the necessity for simulations over extended time spans to obtain statistically relevant data, and the nonlinearities inherent in the Equations of Motion (EOMs).
elements.sent-2411-013: In fact, the pursue of light-design structures leads to large in service structural displacements whose behavior can be accurately modelled only by assuming finite rotations in the FE formulation.
elements.sent-2411-014: As a result, geometric nonlinearities are introduced in the FE model within the Total Lagrangian formulation, or with the adoption of von K\'arm\'an strains in thin walled structures undergoing mild deformations.
elements.sent-2411-015: The core principle behind model reduction is the replacement of solutions of the starting FE model (referred to as High Fidelity Model (HFM)) with ROM counterparts which require very affordable computational effort.
elements.sent-2411-016: Among different approaches, Galerkin Projection (GP) is arguably the most classical and established technique for model reduction of structural dynamics problems, enjoying extensive application in academia as well as in an industrial setting.
elements.sent-2411-017: In GP ROMs, dimensionality reduction is achieved by restricting the HFM solution space to a small linear subspace spanned by few vectors constituting a reduction basis (RB).
elements.sent-2411-018: Different approaches, generally classifiable in data-driven and pyhsics-based strategies, can be followed for RB construction.
elements.sent-2411-019: Data-driven strategies rely on the availability of HFM solutions from which relevant vector components are usually extracted using Proper Orthogonal Decomposition (POD) \cite{krysl2001dimensional,kerschen2007physical,lieu2005pod and stacked together to form the RB.}
elements.sent-2411-020: This comes at the large cost of running HFM simulations.
elements.sent-2411-021: On the other hand, physics-based approaches are simulation free and harness information from the physics modelled in the equations to construct the RB.
elements.sent-2411-022: Among these techniques, Modal Analysis \cite{geradin2015mechanical represents the cornerstone for model reduction of linear structural systems and acts as the foundation for extending reduction to nonlinear systems.}
elements.sent-2411-023: As such, the RB for geometrically nonlinear structures can be constructed by complementing the RB of the underlying linearized system with additional vectors that capture the most prominent nonlinear effects.
elements.sent-2411-024: To this end, Modal Derivatives vectors (MDs) \cite{Idelsohn1985,idelsohn1985load have been effectively utilized for model reduction of a large array of structures, both for transient and forced response \cite{Marconi2020,Marconi2021,Morteza,Sombroek2018,Wu2016,Rutzmoser2017,tiso2011reduction .}}
elements.sent-2411-025: However, for nonlinear systems, the speed up in time integration of the ROM with respect to the HFM is generally small \cite{Farhat2014,Rutzmoser2017,Jain2018,An2008,rutzmoserThesis , since another computational bottleneck arises: the construction of the reduced internal force vector and of its Jacobian.}
elements.sent-2411-026: For FE structures with geometric nonlinearities and linear elastic material, this bottleneck can be easily addressed by exploiting the polynomial nature of the internal forces \cite{Mignolet2008, mignolet2013review .}
elements.sent-2411-027: In an exact formulation, the reduced forces and their Jacobian are expressed as third order polynomials in the reduced coordinates and are be cheaply evaluated during time integration through tensor contraction operations.
elements.sent-2411-028: Conversely, the computation of tensors associated to reduced forces is performed once and for all offline, i.e. during model construction prior to time integration.
elements.sent-2411-029: The direct methods, were the first to be used \cite{nashThesis, shi1996finite, almroth1978automatic and rely on the knowledge of the decomposition of the internal forces into their linear, quadratic and cubic components.}
elements.sent-2411-030: This separation is generally not provided by commercial FE packages like ABAQUS, NASTRAN or ANSYS, and as such, had limited the use of tensorial formulation to ROM constructed from ad-hoc written FE codes.
elements.sent-2411-031: This limitation was overcome in \cite{Muravyov2003,McEwan2001,Hollkamp2008 with the introduction of indirect methods, where tensors are identified using the FE code as a black-box, returning forces to displacements inputs and displacements to forces inputs.}
elements.sent-2411-032: The most popular indirect methods for tensor identification are the Implicit Condensation (IC)\cite{McEwan2001 , Implicit Condensation and Expansion (ICE)\cite{Hollkamp2008 and Enforced Displacement (ED)\cite{Muravyov2003 .}}}
elements.sent-2411-033: Models for acoustic load predictions of aerospace components fall usually into this category, since the high modal density and the large excitation bandwidth of the load force the analyst to include a large number of vectors in the RB \cite{Perez2014 .}
elements.sent-2411-034: The Enhanced Enforced Displacement (EED) was proposed in this context \cite{Perez2014 .}
elements.sent-2411-035: The EED cuts the computational cost of ED tensor identification through the use of the tangent stiffness matrix instead of the internal force vector only.
elements.sent-2411-036: Even with EED, tensor construction remains the computational bottleneck in the construction of large physics-based ROMs, potentially constituting a major issue when multiple realizations are needed.
elements.sent-2411-037: This scenario can be envisioned in geometry optimization problems or in uncertainty quantification in presence of geometrical defects \cite{Wang2018,Perez2011 .}
elements.sent-2411-038: Within these methods we mention Discrete Empirical Interpolation (DEIM) \cite{chaturantabut2010nonlinear and Energy Conserving Sampling and Weighting (ECSW) \cite{Farhat2014,Farhat2015 .}}
elements.sent-2411-039: However, both DEIM and ECSW reduced forces approximation is constructed from training forces that are generally obtained from HFM simulations and could compromise ROM offline construction time.
elements.sent-2411-040: Simulation-free strategies have been proposed in \cite{Rutzmoser2017, Jain2018 for ECSW only.}
elements.sent-2411-041: Specifically, ECSW was trained in \cite{Rutzmoser2017,rutzmoserThesis with nonlinear static solutions, whereas in \cite{Jain2018 with displacements obtained from a linear run lifted on a second order nonlinear manifold.}}
elements.sent-2411-042: The drawback of ECSW-ROMs lies, however, in their intrusive nature: during solution of the ROMs equations, internal element forces and jacobians are computed with the FE code.
elements.sent-2411-043: This fact does not constitute a problem if the numerical integration scheme and ROM construction are embedded in the FE package, while it becomes a major limitation when the FE formulation is not accessible.
elements.sent-2411-044: This is usually the case when ROMs equipped with ECSW hyperreduction rely on commercial FE packages: the communication cost between ROM solver and FE code severely undermines online ROM performance as recently reported in \cite{Trainotti2024 .}
elements.sent-2411-045: Our approach is based on EED identification procedure and ECSW trained with the second order manifold as in \cite{Jain2018 .}
elements.sent-2411-046: The idea is to cheaply construct a hyperreduced mesh to use for a fast evaluation of the tangent stiffness in the EED scheme.
elements.sent-2411-047: Our method shares similarities with the recent work proposed in \cite{Kim2023 .}
elements.sent-2411-048: In that work, an ECSW reduced mesh was constructed following the same approach proposed in \cite{Jain2018 and used to speed up tensor construction of a parametric ROM within a direct identification scheme.}
elements.sent-2411-049: In this work we propose a similar strategy based on ECSW for efficient tensor identification, coupled however with an indirect identification scheme.
elements.sent-2411-050: This great distinctive feature of the method here proposed from the one presented in \cite{Kim2023 , allows for efficient tensor construction using commercial FE packages, rendering the method applicable in an industrial setting.}
elements.sent-2411-051: The equations of motion for FE models of thin-walled structures with geometric nonlinearities and linear elastic material read \begin{equation}\M \qdd + \C\qd + \fI (\q) = \fe(t), \label{eq:eomF}\end{equation} where $\q \in \R^{n}$ is the vector of nodal displacements, $\M, \C \in \R^{n \times n}$ are the mass and viscous damping matrices, $\fI(\q), \fe \in \R^n$ are, respectively, the vectors of internal and external forces.
elements.sent-2411-052: The tangent stiffness matrix $\Ktg \in \R^{n \times n}$ is given as \begin{equation}\Ktg = \Pder{\fI}{\q}.\end{equation}.
elements.sent-2411-053: In these models, the kinematic strain-displacements relations are based on the Green-Lagrange strain tensor or on the approximate von K\'arm\'an strains \cite{crisfield1991 .}
elements.sent-2411-054: This leads to an internal force vector $\fI(\q)$ that is an exact cubic polynomial function of the nodal displacements $\q$ \cite{Mignolet2008 , which can be be written in tensorial and Einstein's notation as \footnote{Unless differently specified, we adopt in the following Einstein's summation convention over repeated indices. \begin{equation}\fI(\q) = \Ko \cdot \q + \Ktw \cddot (\q \otimes \q) + \Kth \cdddot\ (\q \otimes \q \otimes \q ) \iff \fIs_{i} = \ko_{ij}\qs_{j} + \ktw_{ijk}\qs_{j}\qs_{k} + \kth_{ijkl} \qs_{j}\qs_{k}\qs_{l}, \label{eq:tensDecF}\end{equation} where $\Ko \in \mathbb{R ^{n\times n}$ is the linear stiffness matrix, $\Ktw \in \mathbb{R ^{n\times n \times n}$ and $\Kth \in \R^{n \times n \times n \times n}$ are respectively the tensors associated to the quadratic and cubic forces, "$\otimes$" is the dyadic product operator, and '$\ \cdot\ $','$\ \cddot\ $','$\ \cdddot\ \ $' denote respectively, single, double and triple contraction.}}}}
elements.sent-2411-055: The tensors $\Ktw$ and $\Kth$ are commonly referred to as nonlinear stiffness operators, while $\Ko$ is the linear stiffness.
elements.sent-2411-056: The tensorial formulation of internal forces in Eq.\eqref{eq:tensDecF can be derived by partial differentiation of a quartic order elastic potential function.}
elements.sent-2411-057: As a result, the FE tensors are symmetric with respect to all axis, i.e. they are invariant to permutations of their indices.
elements.sent-2411-059: As will be shown in the sequel, this decomposition of internal forces is essential for efficient model reduction.
elements.sent-2411-060: In the following, Eq. \eqref{eq:eomF is referred to as HFM.}
elements.sent-2411-061: Note that the method we propose could also be applied when other formulations, as for instance co-rotational, are adopted.
elements.sent-2411-062: In this case, the form \eqref{eq:tensDecF is an approximation of the actual non-polynomial reduced order forces.}
elements.sent-2411-063: In GP ROMs, model reduction is achieved by restricting the HFM solution to a small linear subspace of the HFM solution space.
elements.sent-2411-064: In particular, it is enforced that \begin{equation}\q(t) \approx \V \qr(t), \label{eq:redBas}\end{equation} where $\V \in \R^{n\times m}$ (with $m \ll n $) is the RB, and $\qr \in \R^{m}$ is the vector of reduced coordinates.
elements.sent-2411-065: After substitution of Eq. \eqref{eq:redBas into Eq. \eqref{eq:eomF , the derived equations are projected onto the reduction basis $\V$, obtaining \begin{equation}\VT\M\V\qrdd + \VT\C\V\qrd + \VT\fI(\V\qr) = \VT\fe, \label{eq:redEqsGP}\end{equation} where we dropped in the notation the dependence on the reduced coordinates on time.}}
elements.sent-2411-066: The ROM equations can be equivalently derived by applying the principle of Virtual Work to the HFM with the constraint in Eq.\eqref{eq:redBas by requiring that the residual $\mathbf{r}$ obtained inserting \eqref{eq:redBas into \eqref{eq:eomF is orthogonal to $\V$, i.e. $\VT \mathbf{r = \zero$.}}}}
elements.sent-2411-067: As a result, the Galerkin ROM is said to preserve the Lagrangian Structure \cite{Farhat2015 associated to the HFM.}
elements.sent-2411-068: Choice of the RB impacts on accuracy and efficiency of the derived ROM: a good RB should span as accurately as possible the HFM solution with a small number of vectors.
elements.subsection-2411-003: Basis construction with Static Modal Derivatives
elements.subsubsection-2411-001: Reduction Basis for Linear Systems: Vibration Modes
elements.sent-2411-069: Physics-based approaches represent an excellent choice for RB selection, since they are computationally cheap, as opposed to data-driven methods.
elements.sent-2411-070: For structural dynamics, the RB is usually constructed starting from a set of Vibration Modes (VMs) of the underlying linearized system.
elements.sent-2411-071: These vectors are computed by solving the following eigenvalue problem: \begin{equation}(-\M \omega_i^2 + \Ko) \eigv_i = \zero,\ \ \ \text{for}\ \ i = 1,...,n, \label{eq:eigLin}\end{equation} where $\eigv_i$ is the $i$\textsuperscript{th VM and $\omega_i$ its corresponding angular frequency.}
elements.sent-2411-072: Strategy for VMs selection depends on the application.
elements.sent-2411-073: For unforced transient analysis, the relevant VMs are the ones with lowest frequencies, since high frequency modes are generally heavily damped.
elements.sent-2411-074: For forced problems, however, VMs should be selected both based on the Modal Participation Factor (MPF) of the load \cite{geradin2015mechanical , and on its frequency content: all the VMs with non zero MPF, whose eigenfrequency is within (or close to) the excitation frequency bandwidth of the load, should be included in the RB.}
elements.subsubsection-2411-002: Reduction Basis for Nonlinear Systems: Static Modal Derivatives
elements.sent-2411-075: Using RBs of only VMs for model reduction of nonlinear structures leads generally to poor results.
elements.sent-2411-076: In fact, some additional vectors, modelling nonlinear effects, should be included in the RB.
elements.sent-2411-077: In thin-walled structures, geometric nonlinearities are due to coupling between out-and in-plane displacements: as structural deformation occurs along out plane modes (which are generally the ones excited by the load) axial stretching arises \cite{tiso20214 .}
elements.sent-2411-078: Modal Derivatives vectors, first presented in \cite{Idelsohn1985,idelsohn1985load and successively employed for reduction of a wide array of structures \cite{Marconi2020,Marconi2021,Morteza,Sombroek2018,Wu2016,Andersson2023 , proved to well capture the bending-stretching coupling.}}
elements.sent-2411-079: These vectors were defined in \cite{Idelsohn1985 as the sensitivities of the eigenvalue problem in Eq. \eqref{eq:eigLin to a change of the linearization point in the directions of the VMs.}}
elements.sent-2411-080: However, only their simplified version, known as Static Modal Derivatives (SMDs)\cite{Jain2017 , have been mostly used for practical model reduction problems.}
elements.sent-2411-081: Therefore, we recall in this section the definition and properties of SMDs only, referring the reader to \cite{Idelsohn1985,Weeger2016 for a detailed discussion on MDs.}
elements.sent-2411-082: The SMD $\bm{\theta _{ij \in \R^n$, related to VMs $\bm{\phi _i,\bm{\phi _j$, is defined as \begin{equation}\Ko\bm{\theta}_{ij} = - \left. \Pderm{\fI(\q)}{\eps_i}{\eps_j} \right|_{\eps_i,\eps_j = 0} , \label{eq:SMDdef}\end{equation} with $\q = \bm{\phi _i\eps_i+\bm{\phi _j\eps_j$.}}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-083: When the decomposition of the internal forces in Eq. \eqref{eq:tensDecF holds, the above definition for SMDs is equivalent to \begin{equation}\Ko\bm{\theta}_{ij} = - \Ktw \cddot (\bm{\phi_i} \otimes \bm{\phi_j}), \label{eq:SMDdef1}\end{equation} and the SMDs share a symmetry property for which $\bm{\theta _{ij = \bm{\theta _{ji}$ \cite{Jain2017 , since the FE tensors are symmetric.}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-084: This allows to construct RB for the nonlinear system by complementing the RB for its linearized counterpart with only $({n_{\phi ^2+{n_{\phi )/2$ vectors, instead of ${n_{\phi ^2$ vectors, being ${n_{\phi }$ the number of retained VMs.}}}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-085: Notice that the number of SMDs grows almost quadratically with the number of VMs.
elements.sent-2411-086: This could lead to large RBs, resulting in inefficient ROMs.
elements.sent-2411-087: To mitigate this problem, a selection strategy for SMDs, based on a linear run, has been proposed in \cite{tiso2011optimal .}
elements.sent-2411-089: For example, when a central difference scheme is adopted, the approximation reads \begin{equation}\left. \Pderm{\fI(\q)}{\eps_i}{\eps_j} \right|_{\eps_i,\eps_j = 0} = \left.\Pder{\Ktg(\q)}{\epsilon_i}\right|_{\eps_i,\eps_j = 0} \bm{\phi}_j \approx \frac{\Ktg{(h\bm{\phi}_i)}-\Ktg{(-h\bm{\phi}_i)}}{2h}\bm{\phi}_j, \label{eq:SMDsRHS}\end{equation} where $h$ is a user defined scalar perturbation parameter.
elements.sent-2411-090: In addition to the classic projection approach, SMDs have been used in a second order manifold for reduction of thin walled structures \cite{Jain2017 .}
elements.sent-2411-091: Loosely speaking, the idea behind the quadratic manifold is to constrain SMDs amplitudes, to the amplitudes of VMs.
elements.sent-2411-092: More specifically, starting with $n_{\phi}$ VMs, the HFM solution is enforced to lay on the quadratic constraint that reads \begin{equation}\q = \bm{\Gamma}(\bm{\gamma}) = \sum_{i=1}^{n_{\phi}} \gamma_i \bm{\phi}_i + \frac{1}{2}\sum_{i=1}^{n_{\phi}}\sum_{j=1}^{n_{\phi}} \gamma_i \gamma_j\bm{\theta}_{ij}, \label{eq:QM}\end{equation} where $\gamma_i$ is the amplitude of VM $\bm{\phi _i$.}
elements.sent-2411-093: In the following, we refer to this manifold as the Static Quadratic Manifold (SQM).\par For a von-K\'arm\'an beam, the SQM is equivalent to the Static Condensation \cite{Jain2017 , a technique in which the axial degrees of freedom are statically constrained to the transverse ones.}
elements.sent-2411-094: Even if model reduction with the SQM proved to work effectively for some flat structures \cite{Jain2017,Rutzmoser2017QM , its application to slightly curved structures can lead to misleading predictions \cite{Vizzaccaro2021 .}}
elements.sent-2411-095: In fact, in more recent work in \cite{Vizzaccaro2021 and \cite{Veraszto2020 it was shown that the SQM is generally not invariant, unless the spectral gap between the frequency of the VMs retained in the expansion and the ones of the other VMs is large.}}
elements.sent-2411-096: For this reason, we use in the following the more robust approach where the SMDs are complementing a RB of VMs.
elements.sent-2411-097: Despite model reduction, integrating the ROM equations as in \eqref{eq:redEqsGP typically incurs a computational cost comparable to that of the HFM.}
elements.sent-2411-098: The reason behind this limited speed-up is the expensive construction of the reduced force vector and of its Jacobian during Newton-Raphson iterations within the solution scheme.

elements.sent-2411-100: As such, internal forces and tangent stiffness matrices must be first constructed using the FE model with element-level and assembly operations, whose computational cost scales with the size of the HFM.

elements.sent-2411-104: Reduced order tensors inherit the same symmetry property of their full counterpart, as described in section \ref{sec:HFMeqs .}
elements.sent-2411-105: Exploiting symmetries enables efficient storage, faster online reduced forces evaluations and provides a metric to assess accuracy of the identified tensors \cite{rutzmoserThesis .\par.}
elements.sent-2411-106: In order to exploit the substantial advantages of the tensorial formulation for time integration, tensors must be correctly identified in the model construction process.
elements.sent-2411-107: Strategies for tensors identification can be classified in two different categories: direct methods and indirect methods \cite{mignolet2013review .}
elements.sent-2411-108: Direct methods were the first to be proposed in \cite{nashThesis, shi1996finite, almroth1978automatic .}
elements.sent-2411-109: Within these intrusive techniques, reduced tensors are computed by direct projection of the full tensors on the RB, performing the operations in Eq. \eqref{eq:tensProj .}
elements.sent-2411-110: As such, direct methods require the knowledge of the full order tensors $\Ko$, $\Ktw$ and $\Kth$, limiting ROM applicability to FE codes that offer this output.
elements.sent-2411-111: This distinctive feature of indirect methods makes them appealing for the solution of industrial problems, that are usually tackled with commercial FE programs.
elements.sent-2411-112: Indirect methods can be further divided in Implicit Condenstation (IC) methods \cite{McEwan2001,Hollkamp2008 and ED methods \cite{Muravyov2003,Perez2014 .}}
elements.sent-2411-113: The former (IC) are used to construct ROMs where the membrane inertia is neglected and membrane contributions to generalized forces are implicitly modelled in the identified tensors.
elements.sent-2411-114: In fact, the RB of IC-ROMs is usually composed of only bending VMs.
elements.sent-2411-115: On the other hand, ED methods are employed in ROMs where the nonlinear stretching effects are explicitly modelled with additional vectors in the RB, such as Dual Modes \cite{Mignolet2008 , MDs and SMDs.}
elements.sent-2411-116: The ED method, also known as STiffness Evaluation Procedure method (STEP), was first put forward in \cite{Muravyov2003 .}
elements.sent-2411-117: This scheme for tensor identification can be broken down in two subsequent steps.
elements.sent-2411-118: In the first step, the FE solver is used to compute nonlinear nodal forces to a set of prescribed displacements in the direction of the vectors of the RB.
elements.sent-2411-119: In the second step, linear algebraic systems of equations are set up with the previously computed forces and solved for the unknown tensor entries.
elements.sent-2411-120: For $m$ vectors in the RB, $(m^3-3m^2+2m)/6$ nonlinear static problems must be solved to fully identify the tensors.
elements.sent-2411-121: For this reason, tensor identification can become prohibitively expensive, as ROM size increases.\par The EED was proposed in \cite{Perez2014 to mitigate the high computational cost of ED.}
elements.sent-2411-122: The Enhanced Enforced Displacements leverages the output of the tangent stiffness matrix to reduce the total number of nonlinear static solutions required for tensor identification.
elements.sent-2411-123: Using EED, tensors can be fully identified from only $(m^2+5m)/2$ nonlinear static solutions.
elements.sent-2411-124: In this section, we present a short review of the EED method, as our new approach for tensor identification here presented is heavily based on it.

elements.subsubsection-2411-017: Convergence analysis for number of VMs in RB
elements.sent-2411-307: In this subsection we present a convergence analysis of the PSD obtained using the ROM, for an increase in the number of VMs retained in the RB.
elements.sent-2411-308: Eight different ROMs were computed for different RBs, subsequently constructed by including VMs with non-zero sMPF, for increasing eigenfrequency.
elements.sent-2411-309: Specifically, we progressively included in the RBs VMs $1,4,8,10,13,17$ and 19, along with their corresponding SMDs, one at a time.
elements.sent-2411-310: Convergence of the PSD of displacement history of node A along $z$ is displayed in Fig. \ref{fig:convAnalysis .}
elements.sent-2411-311: It is worth to notice that multiple VMs are needed for a correct representation of the PSD over all the frequency span.
elements.sent-2411-312: In particular, addition of single VMs improves the PSD results in the neighbourhood of the mode eigenfrequency.
elements.sent-2411-313: The requirement of multiple VMs in the RB for correct PSD estimation leads to large ROMs whose computation is generally intensive, hence justifying the need for the efficient ROM identification scheme herein presented.

elements.sent-2411-314: As a second test case we present the application of our method to a model of the aircraft sidewall fuselage panel experimentally investigated in \cite{buehrle2000finite .}
elements.sent-2411-315: The nine bay panel consists of a skin panel reinforced with a frame and a longeron substructure dividing the skin into nine bays.
elements.sent-2411-316: The response of this panel to acoustic loading was assessed in \cite{przekop2006nonlinear and \cite{Perez2014 using projection ROMs.}}
elements.sent-2411-317: In the first study \cite{przekop2006nonlinear , the RB was constructed using only VMs, with tensors identified through ED.}
elements.sent-2411-318: In contrast, the second study \cite{Perez2014 included Dual Modes in the RB, and EED was used for tensor identification.}
elements.sent-2411-319: The FE model that we used is derived from the one employed in \cite{przekop2006nonlinear by refining the mesh, and by substituting beam elements connecting the shell surfaces with strings of shell elements.}
elements.sent-2411-320: Mesh refining was performed to demonstrate the applicability of the proposed approach to large FE models, while the substitution of beam elements with shells was intended to create a FE model composed of elements of the same type, simplifying the implementation.
elements.sent-2411-321: However, the proposed method for ROM construction could also be applicable to FE models featuring different element types.
elements.sent-2411-322: The adopted linear elastic material has Young's modulus $E = 72.395 \ \text{GPa}$, Poisson's ratio $\nu = 0.33$ and density $\rho = 2794 \ \text{Kg/m ^3$.}
elements.sent-2411-323: The FE model was constructed with Abaqus, using $33,208$ S4R elements and features $200,886$ unconstrained degrees of freedom.
elements.sent-2411-324: A sketch of the FE mesh is provided in figure \ref{fig:meshNineBay .a.}
elements.sent-2411-325: The nine-bay panel is constrained by blocking all the displacements degrees of freedom at the edges of the skin, leaving the nodal rotations free.
elements.sent-2411-326: The applied load is a uniform pressure in space, varying randomly in time as a band limited white noise, acting on the skin of the panel.
elements.sent-2411-327: The load was constructed as described in section \ref{sec:curvedPanel for the curved panel, imposing an OASPL of $147$ dB and a cut-off frequency of $500$ Hz.}
elements.sent-2411-328: Rayleigh damping model was adopted, with values of $\alpha$ and $\beta$ determined by imposing a damping ratio of $0.01$ to the first two structural VMs.
elements.sent-2411-329: The first $90$ VMs and natural frequencies were computed with Abaqus.
elements.sent-2411-330: Modal selection for this problem is crucial, since $76$ VMs fall within the frequency bandwidth up to the cut off frequency of the load (Fig.\ref{fig:sMPFNineBay .b), and their blind inclusion in the RB would result in an inefficient ROM.}
elements.sent-2411-331: As such, the RB was constructed from the 15 VMs with the highest sMPF (see Fig.\ref{fig:sMPFNineBay .a).}
elements.sent-2411-332: Subsequently, SMDs were computed non-intrusively from these VMs, and ranked based on a newly proposed criteria that builds on the same spirit of the selection strategy proposed in \cite{tiso2011optimal .}
elements.sent-2411-333: In particular, the SMPFs of the VMs included in the RBs were cross multiplied, obtaining \begin{equation}{\text{MDPF}}_{ij} = \text{SMPF}_{i} \cdot \text{SMPF}_{j} \quad \text{for}\ \ i \geq j,\end{equation} where $i,j$ are indices corresponding to the VMs in the RBs, and $\text{MDPF _{ij}$ is name the Modal Derivative Participation Factor of the $i$\textsuperscript{th and $j$\textsuperscript{th VMs.}}}
elements.sent-2411-334: The rationale behind the design of this ranking measure is that the sMPF for a given VM is assumed to be proportional to the modal displacements of that VM when the load is applied to the linearized system.
elements.sent-2411-335: If this holds, this ranking criteria for SMDs is similar to the selection criteria proposed in \cite{tiso2011optimal .}
elements.sent-2411-336: Based on this, we form the RB by adding to the selected $15$ VMs the $35$ SMDs with the highest values of MDPF index.

elements.subsubsection-2411-020: Effect of training ECSW tolerance on accuracy and speed-up
elements.sent-2411-366: The choice of the relative tolerance value $\tau$ to use in ECSW training is driven by experience of the analyst.
elements.sent-2411-367: In this subsection we illustrate how the relative tolerance impacts on the performance of the ROM, both in terms of construction efficiency and prediction accuracy.
elements.sent-2411-368: To this end, we identified ROM tensors with EED-ECSW using different values of relative tolerance $\tau = 0.01,0.005,0.001,0.0005,0.0001$.
elements.sent-2411-369: As expected, we obtained different reduced meshes consisting of different number of elements, affecting the reduced mesh validation error $\epsilon_{\text{ECSW }$ defined in \eqref{eq:ecswErr and the computational time for the ensuing tensor identification.}}
elements.sent-2411-370: The result of this experiment are reported in table \ref{tensIdTable .}
elements.sent-2411-371: The speed up in tensor identification obtainable using EED-ECSW instead of EED is inversely proportional to values of the relative tolerance $\tau$.

elements.sent-2411-375: We introduced a new procedure to accelerate the EED scheme for non-intrusive identification of nonlinear stiffness tensors in projection based ROM with RB of VMs and MDs.
elements.sent-2411-376: The speed up is achieved by replacing the evaluations of the reduced tangent stiffness matrix with fast-to-compute approximations based on ECSW.
elements.sent-2411-377: Reduced mesh and associated weights are obtained using a simulation-free approach where training displacements snapshots are generated from quasi-random samples of the parametrization coordinates of the SQM.
elements.sent-2411-378: Reduced Order Model tensors are firstly identified in a physical RB using EED and subsequently transformed to their counterparts associated to an orthogonalized RB.
elements.sent-2411-379: This step is essential to avoid imposing non-physical displacements to the FE model.
elements.sent-2411-380: The proposed methodology was tested on two different case studies: a shallow rectangular curved panel and a reinforced nine-bay panel subjected to random acoustic loading.
elements.sent-2411-381: In both these applications, the broad-bandwidth nature of the load triggers many structural modes, resulting in large ROMs whose tensors are expensive to construct.
elements.sent-2411-382: Dynamic response was investigated using a ROM with tensors identified using standard EED, the same ROM with approximated tensors identified using EED-ECSW, the HFM and a linear ROM.
elements.sent-2411-383: Power spectral densities of displacements computed from simulations performed with ROM equipped with approximated tensors are in excellent agreement with the ones computed using the exact tensor ROM and the HFM.
elements.sent-2411-384: Nonlinear effects, such as smearing and shifting of resonance peaks and out-of-band response are well captured by the nonlinear models (ROMs and HFM), whereas they are missed by the linearized ROM.
elements.sent-2411-385: The proposed approach for efficient tensor identification allowed for a remarkable reduction of the computational time for ROM construction when compared to standard EED.
elements.sent-2411-386: Speed ups of 3.18 and 13.86 in ROM construction were recorded for the curved panel and the nine-bay panel respectively.
elements.sent-2411-387: The provided test cases show that the proposed method is appealing for the design of industrial structural components in which different ROMs have to be constructed for different tested geometries, requiring therefore efficient ROM construction.
elements.sent-2411-388: We here prove that the SMPFs introduced in Eq. \eqref{eq:MPF are the linear combination coefficients of the linear static solution to load vector $\mathbf{p}$, for unit normalized modes.}
elements.sent-2411-390: These equations can be compactly written as \begin{equation}\qrdds_i + \omega_i^2 \qrs_i = \frac{1}{\mu_i}\vmT_i \mathbf{p} ,\quad \text{for} \ i \in \{1,...,n\},\end{equation} where $\mu_i = \vmT_i \M \vm_i$ and $\omega_i$ are respectively the modal mass and angular frequency of mode $i$.
elements.sent-2411-391: By plugging the static solution to load vector $\mathbf{p}$ \begin{equation}\qrs_i = \frac{1}{\mu_i \omega_i^2}\vmT_i \mathbf{p} = \frac{1}{\vm_i^T\mathbf{K}\vm_i}\vmT_i \mathbf{p}\end{equation} in the expansion \eqref{eq:provExp we get \begin{equation}\q = \sum_{i=1}^{n} \frac{\vm_i}{\|\vm_i\|_2} \left( \frac{\|\vm_i\|_2}{\vm_i^T\mathbf{K}\vm_i}\vmT_i \mathbf{p} \right) = \sum_{i=1}^{n} \frac{\vm_i}{\|\vm_i\|_2} \text{sMPF}_i\end{equation} where we unit normalized the VMs in the RB, by dividing and multiplying vectors $\vm_i$ by their 2 norm.}
